title: A list that is not a list
statement:
Run the code. You see a list. Three things, one under another, each with a dash in front.

The browser sees one paragraph.

Make it what it looks like.

examples.explain: The dashes are gone. The browser draws the bullets, because this is now actually a list.

constraints: The words stay as they are given to you
Nothing is left over from the old code

checks.t9: The items are in a list

why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted everything, the page included.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
why.not-closed:
A tag is open and never closed. Every
  • wants a
    • .
why.lost: You lost some of the content. The three things on the list stay three.
why.not-a-list: There are still no list items. A dash is a sign YOU can see. To the browser it is just a letter, like an a or a b.
why.empty-li: There is a list item with no text in it. An empty box counts for nothing.
why.no-ul: The items are there, but the list is not. An <li> means a thing from a list — from which list? The browser draws the bullets even without the container, and that is exactly what fools you. It looks right. It is not.
why.old-paragraph:
The paragraph is still there. You may have put the list INSIDE it — but a list inside a paragraph is not allowed. The browser silently closes the paragraph before the list and throws the list out. You wrote one paragraph, you got two empty ones — and neither holds the list.

The paragraph does not wrap the list. The paragraph goes.
why.old-breaks: There are line break tags left over. A list puts each thing on its own line by itself — it needs no help.
why.dashes-left: The dashes are still in the text. Now you have a list that draws a bullet, and next to it you wrote another dash. Twice over.

not-a-list: You see a list. The browser sees one paragraph with dashes inside. Which of you is right?
A similar case: "- monday - tuesday" inside a paragraph is text. Three list items in one container are a list. They look the same.
The paragraph goes. In its place comes a container, and each thing inside it is its own item.
no-ul: List items do not live alone. They live in something.
Think: if you have three list items side by side, how does the browser know they are ONE list and not three separate ones?
The items get wrapped in a container. One container. Three items inside it.
dashes-left: Look at the bullets in the preview. Their number tells you what happened.
The bullet in front is drawn by the browser. You wrote another one.
old-paragraph: Search with Ctrl+F for the paragraph tag. It is still there.
You made the list but did not remove what it replaces.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to put dots and dashes so it looks like a list. But a dash is a sign for the eye — to the browser it is just a letter. A list that looks like a list but is not one is the most common mistake here.

The second trap is sneakier: you make <li> but forget <ul>. The browser still draws the bullets, and you think you are done. You are not — items without a container are not a list, they only look like one.
steps: Remove the paragraph. It is not a container for a list — a list has its own.
Open a <ul>. It says: a list starts here.

Wrap each thing in
  • and
    • . That is one item of the list.
Remove the dashes. The bullet in front is drawn by <ul> itself — yours is redundant.
solution.code:
What I need from the shop

  • salt
  • pepper
  • two lemons
solution.note: Do not look at whether it looks right. Look at whether it IS right. The browser draws bullets even on a broken list — the eye lies, the structure does not.
